explicit_text: I was born in Budapest in 1920 and grew up there during a turbulent time. My parents were Jewish but converted to Catholicism before I was born, so I was raised Catholic. I studied mathematics and philosophy, but due to the political uncertainty, I earned a degree in pharmacology to work in my father's pharmacy. After World War II, I earned a doctorate in philosophy from the University of Budapest and worked as an assistant professor of sociology there until the late 1940s.

After World War II, I earned a doctorate in philosophy from the main university in that city and worked as an assistant professor of sociology there until the late 1940s.

explicit_text: During World War II, after Germany occupied Hungary, I was drafted into a forced-labor unit. Near the war's end, I managed to escape deportation by convincing a guard at a railway station that I was there to support a Jewish friend. Only I and three others who jumped from the train survived from our labor unit. The war deeply affected my life and shaped my later decisions.

explicit_text: Hungary was my homeland where I was born and raised, and where I lived through World War II. After the war, I earned my doctorate and taught sociology at the University of Budapest. However, in the late 1940s, my views clashed with the Communist government, leading me to leave my academic post and return to work in my father's pharmacy. In 1950, I escaped Hungary with my future wife, Anne, crossing into Austria before moving on to Australia.

entity: United States Arms Control and Disarmament Agency
explicit_text: In 1964, I was invited to be one of ten game theorists advising the United States Arms Control and Disarmament Agency on negotiations with the Soviet Union. The other advisers felt they could not help due to the lack of information on both sides, but I developed a method to assign probabilities to possible moves and outcomes. This work was a practical application of my game theory research and had implications for international negotiations.
